united_states.name: United States
united_states.summary:
In 1945, the United States emerges from World War II as the undisputed dominant power of the Western world — and arguably the world. Its industrial base is intact (unlike every major European and Asian combatant), its economy has been supercharged by wartime production, and it alone possesses the atomic bomb. The United States in 1945 produces roughly 50% of world GDP.

The GI generation returns home to the suburbs, the baby boom, and unprecedented prosperity. The Marshall Plan will rebuild Europe, the United Nations (based in New York) attempts a new world order, and the Cold War with the Soviet Union is already beginning. America's global role — superpower, world's policeman, defender of liberal democracy — is being born in this moment.
united_states.ruler: Harry S. Truman (r. 1945–1953); FDR died April 12, 1945
key_events: FDR dies (April 12, 1945); Truman orders atomic bombs dropped on Japan
V-E Day (May 8) and V-J Day (September 2) end the war
US produces roughly 50% of world GDP in 1945
United Nations Charter signed (June 1945) in San Francisco
united_states.did_you_know: The United States in 1945 had the world's only operational atomic weapons, the largest navy ever assembled, and produced half the world's economic output. No single nation had dominated the world to this degree since Rome — and America's dominance was arguably greater, given the global scale of 20th-century civilization.

soviet_union.name: Soviet Union
soviet_union.summary:
The Soviet Union has won the war against Nazi Germany at almost unimaginable cost. Between 26 and 27 million Soviet citizens are dead — soldiers and civilians alike — a loss so staggering it exceeds the total population of many European countries. Every family has lost someone. The Western Front was decisive, but the Eastern Front, where Germany and the USSR fought the largest land battles in history, was the war's true killing ground.

Stalin emerges from the war as one of history's most powerful individuals, controlling an empire from Berlin to Vladivostok. Soviet armies occupy Eastern Europe and will impose communist governments across it. The Cold War — already beginning even as victory celebrations continue — will define global politics for the next 45 years.
key_events: ~27 million Soviet war deaths — the highest toll of any WWII combatant
Soviet armies occupy Eastern Europe — Iron Curtain descends
Soviet atomic bomb program begins — nuclear parity achieved in 1949
Stalin's Eastern European empire replaces Nazi occupation with Soviet occupation
soviet_union.did_you_know: The Soviet Union's war losses are almost incomprehensible: 27 million dead represents approximately 14% of its pre-war population. The United States, by comparison, lost 418,000 — terrible, but proportionally 65 times less. This disparity fundamentally shaped each country's understanding of the war and their postwar priorities.

united_kingdom.name: United Kingdom
united_kingdom.summary:
Britain has won the war — Churchill's 'finest hour' is real — but victory has come at enormous cost. Britain is effectively bankrupt, having spent its imperial reserves to finance six years of total war. The United States has replaced Britain as the world's dominant power. The British Empire, which Churchill was determined to preserve, is already beginning its rapid dissolution.

In a stunning electoral upset, Churchill is voted out of office in July 1945 — while the war in the Pacific is still ongoing — replaced by Clement Attlee's Labour government. The British public, victorious but exhausted, chooses domestic reconstruction over imperial glory. The welfare state — the National Health Service, social housing, nationalized industries — is about to be built.
united_kingdom.population_estimate: ~49 million in Britain; ~500 million across the empire
united_kingdom.ruler: Churchill until July 1945; then Clement Attlee
key_events: Britain wins WWII but emerges bankrupt and dependent on US loans
Churchill voted out — Labour wins in landslide (July 1945)
National Health Service established (1948) — legacy of wartime sacrifice
Indian independence begins (1947) — the empire's accelerating dissolution
united_kingdom.did_you_know: Churchill was voted out of office on July 26, 1945, while attending the Potsdam Conference — he had to fly home mid-conference after learning the results. He was replaced by Attlee, who then returned to Potsdam. Stalin reportedly couldn't believe a wartime leader of such stature could be removed by an election.

china.name: Republic of China
china.summary:
China has endured the most devastating foreign invasion in its history — Japan's occupation from 1937–1945 killed between 15–20 million Chinese and devastated the country's economy and infrastructure. The Nationalist government of Chiang Kai-shek, operating from the wartime capital Chongqing, survived through determined resistance and Allied support.

Victory over Japan brings not peace but the resumption of civil war between Chiang's Nationalists and Mao Zedong's Communists. The Communists emerge stronger — having fought an effective guerrilla war in rural areas — while the Nationalists are exhausted and corrupt. By 1949, Mao will proclaim the People's Republic of China, and Chiang will retreat to Taiwan.
key_events: Japan surrenders September 2, 1945 — China's 8-year war ends
15–20 million Chinese war dead, including the Nanjing Massacre (300,000 killed in 1937)
Civil war with Communists immediately resumes after Japanese defeat
Mao's Communists will win — Chiang retreats to Taiwan in 1949
china.did_you_know: The Rape of Nanjing (December 1937 – January 1938) saw Japanese forces massacre an estimated 200,000–300,000 Chinese civilians and prisoners of war over six weeks. It remains a source of deep historical grievance between China and Japan and is still politically contentious today.

france.name: France (Liberated)
france.summary:
France has endured a traumatic four years of German occupation — collaboration with the Vichy regime, the deportation of 75,000 Jewish French citizens to Nazi death camps, and the moral ambiguity that came with living under Nazi rule. Liberation in 1944, led by the Free French forces under de Gaulle alongside Allied armies, has restored French sovereignty and national pride.

Charles de Gaulle's genius was to insist — against all evidence — that France was not a defeated nation but a great power that had been temporarily subdued. By insisting on this narrative, France secured a permanent seat on the UN Security Council and a zone of occupation in Germany. The myth of French resistance obscured the reality of widespread collaboration, a reckoning France would struggle with for decades.
key_events: Liberation of Paris (August 1944) — de Gaulle marches down the Champs-Élysées
75,000 French Jews deported to Nazi death camps with Vichy cooperation
France gets a permanent UN Security Council seat — de Gaulle's diplomatic triumph
French Fourth Republic established — beginning decades of political instability
france.did_you_know: France is one of five permanent members of the UN Security Council — alongside the US, Soviet Union/Russia, UK, and China. France's inclusion was largely due to de Gaulle's insistence on France's great-power status and Churchill's support, despite the fact that France had been occupied and its government had collaborated with the Nazis.

germany_usa.name: Germany (Occupied)
germany_usa.summary:
Germany in May 1945 is a nation of rubble. Allied bombing has devastated every major city; the final Soviet assault on Berlin killed 80,000 Soviet soldiers and an estimated 100,000–125,000 German civilians. Hitler commits suicide on April 30; the unconditional surrender follows on May 8 (V-E Day). The Nazi state has ceased to exist.

Germany is divided into four occupation zones controlled by the US, Soviet Union, Britain, and France. Berlin, deep within the Soviet zone, is similarly divided. This arrangement — intended as temporary — will harden into two separate German states: West Germany (Federal Republic) and East Germany (German Democratic Republic), separated by the Iron Curtain for 45 years.
germany_usa.population_estimate: ~66 million (including expelled Germans from Eastern Europe)
germany_usa.ruler: Allied Control Council (US, UK, USSR, France)
key_events: Hitler's suicide (April 30, 1945); Germany surrenders (May 8, V-E Day)
Nuremberg Trials — first international tribunal for war crimes
Germany divided into four Allied occupation zones
12 million ethnic Germans expelled from Eastern Europe in largest peacetime population transfer
germany_usa.did_you_know: The Nuremberg Trials (1945–46) established the legal precedent that 'following orders' is not a defense for war crimes and crimes against humanity — a principle now embedded in international law and the Geneva Conventions. The concept of 'crimes against humanity' as a legal category was invented at Nuremberg.

japan_usa.name: Japan (Occupied)
japan_usa.summary:
Imperial Japan's surrender on September 2, 1945 — following atomic bombings of Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9) that together killed 110,000–210,000 people — ends World War II. Japan is the first and only nation ever subjected to nuclear attack.

General Douglas MacArthur presides over the American occupation, which transforms Japan with remarkable speed. A new pacifist constitution is imposed, the emperor retains symbolic status but renounces divinity, war crimes trials (Tokyo Trials) are held, and land reform redistributes property. Japan's postwar economic miracle — rising from utter devastation to the world's second largest economy by 1968 — is one of history's most extraordinary recoveries.
japan_usa.ruler: Emperor Hirohito (symbolic); General Douglas MacArthur (Supreme Commander)
key_events: Hiroshima (Aug 6) and Nagasaki (Aug 9) — first use of atomic weapons in war
Japan surrenders September 2, 1945 — end of WWII
MacArthur's occupation transforms Japan: new constitution, land reform, war crimes trials
Emperor Hirohito renounces divinity in 'New Year's Jewel Voice' radio broadcast
japan_usa.did_you_know: Emperor Hirohito's surrender broadcast on August 15, 1945 was the first time most Japanese had ever heard their emperor's voice — he had been considered literally divine. He announced defeat in such archaic, formal Japanese that many listeners couldn't understand the words, only that something extraordinary had happened.
